aliases: Roasted red pepper and eggplant relish

english: Roasted red pepper and eggplant relish

region_of_origin: Western Balkans (Serbia, North Macedonia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Croatia)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for ajvar. FOODON_03311737 (pepper sauce food product) is the closest defensible parent class covering roasted red pepper-based condiments.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated ajvar term. A00LC (pepper sauces and relishes) is the closest defensible parent category within the EFSA FoodEx2 classification.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept exists for ajvar as a specific food product. Not clinically coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical condiment serving of ajvar is approximately 50 g (about 2–3 tablespoons), as served alongside grilled meats, flatbreads, or as a dip.

nutrient_source.title: Ajvar, red pepper relish — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for ajvar as a named product. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for roasted red bell peppers (FDC ID 170108) and roasted eggplant (FDC ID 169228), combined in a typical ajvar formulation ratio of approximately 70% roasted red pepper, 20% roasted eggplant, 8% sunflower oil, and 2% garlic, salt, and vinegar. Energy and macros are calculated from component data. This is a medium-confidence estimate. Sodium reflects typical commercial ajvar formulations reported in USDA Branded Food entries for comparable pepper relish products.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of non-starchy vegetables and pepper-based condiments — analogous estimate based on roasted red peppers
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ajvar in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 15 based on the GI of roasted red bell peppers (GI approximately 15, low category) as the dominant ingredient (~70% by weight). Eggplant has a similarly low GI (~15). The added oil further suppresses glycemic response. Glycemic load per 50 g serving = (15 × 8.0 × 0.50) / 100 = 0.6, classified as very low. This estimate is consistent with non-starchy vegetable condiment GI values documented in the University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.guidance: Ajvar contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 tablespoons (25–50 g) per meal to keep sodium contribution below 160 mg, and should choose low-sodium commercial variants where available.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ajvar has an estimated glycemic index of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of less than 1 per standard serving, making it a suitable condiment for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in typical condiment portions of 25–50 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Unsaturated fat profile and antioxidant-rich ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ajvar is low in saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g) and rich in antioxidants from roasted red peppers including vitamin C and vitamin A, supporting a heart-healthy dietary pattern when used as a condiment in place of higher-fat or higher-sodium sauces.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ajvar is a traditional Balkan roasted red pepper and eggplant relish originating from the Western Balkans, particularly Serbia, North Macedonia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, and Croatia. It is made by roasting red bell peppers and eggplant over an open flame or in an oven until charred and soft, then peeling, blending, and slow-cooking the mixture with sunflower oil, garlic, salt, and sometimes vinegar to produce a thick, spreadable condiment. The flavour is sweet, smoky, and mildly tangy, with a smooth to slightly chunky texture depending on preparation style. Ajvar is typically served as a condiment alongside grilled meats such as cevapi and pljeskavica, as a spread on bread, or as a dip. It is a staple of Balkan cuisine and is widely produced commercially across the region and exported globally. Nutritionally, ajvar is a low-calorie, low-glycemic condiment providing approximately 75 kcal per 100 g, with modest fat from sunflower oil (predominantly unsaturated), low protein, and moderate carbohydrate from the peppers. It is a notable source of vitamin C (approximately 48 mg per 100 g) and vitamin A from the red peppers. Sodium content is moderate at approximately 320 mg per 100 g in commercial preparations, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. In Singapore, ajvar is served at Middle Eastern and Balkan-influenced restaurants as an accompaniment to grilled meat platters and mezze-style dishes.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in ajvar?
Can I eat ajvar if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of ajvar?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for ajvar exists in any primary approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central named product). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for roasted red peppers and roasted eggplant in a typical ajvar formulation ratio. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant ingredient (roasted red bell peppers) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (roasted red peppers FDC ID 170108, roasted eggplant FDC ID 169228) combined in a typical ajvar ratio; no exact named ajvar entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or other approved primary sources. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — ajvar is a plant-based product containing no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero). 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no vitamin D is present in plant-based pepper and eggplant relish; naturally zero). 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — sunflower oil used in traditional ajvar contains negligible trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA component data). 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — ajvar is fully plant-based; cholesterol is naturally zero). 6. monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 documented: value 2.8 is estimated from sunflower oil component; reported as numeric estimate. 7. GI set to 15 as analogous estimate based on roasted red bell peppers from University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category low. 8. Sodium of 320 mg per 100 g reflects typical commercial ajvar formulations; home-made versions may have lower sodium. 9. The input record listed cuisine as 'Middle Eastern' reflecting the field survey data restaurant context (Sahara Bar & Restaurant); however, ajvar is a Balkan condiment and cuisine has been corrected to 'Balkan / Eastern European' with region_of_origin updated accordingly. 10. Food name corrected from 'Ajvar only (small)' (menu-style name with portion descriptor) to canonical English name 'Ajvar'. 11. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the USDA component estimation approach used; these are not confirmed zero values but rather not separately derivable from the component-level estimation method.
